entry.title: Stay on top of regulatory changes in SAP Business Network (and more...)
entry.content:
For SAP customers who want to keep an eye on how the products they use adapt to regulatory changes, the SAP Regulatory Change Manager is an easy tool to use and explore. As e-invoicing regulations keep evolving around the globe, the Regulatory Change Manager is now accessible to all SAP Business Network trading partners, so they can gain information on the mandate support per country for SAP Business Network. 

Using the app is pretty straightforward: 

  1. Go to https://app.main.regulatory-change-manager.cloud.sap/,
  2. Navigate to the All Regulatory Changes tab,
  3. Select "SAP Business Network for Procurement" as product, and choose the country/countries relevant to you,
  4. Click the "Go" button,
  5. Click on the result line for accessing information.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Signing up as user

For security reasons you need to be logged in with an SAP ID to access the tool. Please use the username and password you're using on sap.com* (if you have one) or create one in a minute by following 3 easy steps. 

Having an SAP ID allows you to save your filters and to subscribe to news and update on your product of interest. 

                    

 

We invite you to pay the Regulatory Change Manager a visit on a regular basis to read how SAP supports upcoming regulations, especially as e-invoicing and other legal mandates keep evolving around the world. 

 

* Please note these credentials are NOT related to the credentials you use to log in to your SAP Business Network supplier account. 

Looking for more resources for suppliers on SAP Business Network? Visit our dedicated topic page to be kept up to date.

entry.title: Re: Exchange Rate Differences Not Generated in Automatic...
entry.content:
Dear @Jessicaaguilar1,

What you’re seeing is a known/expected behavior when a specific setting is active for the paying company code in the Automatic Payment process Manage Automatic Payments app F0770. In Manage Automatic Payments, SAP can be configured to take the local-currency amount from the cleared open item instead of translating the payment again on the payment posting date. In that case no realized FX gain/loss line is generated, even if the payment date differs from the invoice posting date. This is described in SAP Note for Public Edition

SAP Notes below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002652016

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002861801

The key configuration:

Go to CBC ID 101001 Set Up Paying Company Codes for Payment Transactions and check whether the indicator No Exchange Rate Difference is set for your paying company code/payment setup

 

  • If that indicator is set, then the automatic payment run will not post realized FX differences it uses the invoice translation
  • If you want realized FX differences at payment posting time, make sure this indicator is NOT set then rerun the proposal/payment

as described in the SAP Notes above

Prerequisite FX:

Once FX differences are allowed, the system must be able to determine the accounts for posting them transaction key KDF etc. SAP points to maintaining this in Public Cloud configuration Automatic Account Determination / Define Accounts for Exchange Rate Differences

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002857528

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Global Healthcare Industry Strategy: A Modular, Data-Driven, Open, and Interoperable Platform
entry.content:
The healthcare industry is at an industry inflection point: Rising patient expectations, persistent margin pressure, workforce shortages, evolving reimbursement, escalating cyber threats, and a wave of technological advances are reshaping how providers operate. In such conditions, one-size-fits-all, monolithic systems cannot keep pace. The organizations that will thrive are building modular, cloud-based platforms where integrated applications, context-rich data, and meaningful AI reinforce each other in a continuous flywheel, running efficiently today while adapting quickly to whatever tomorrow brings.

Across the sector, four realities now define the agenda:

  1. AI accelerates and touches nearly everything;
  2. business transformation never stops and needs to prove its value continuously;
  3. patient journeys rarely begin or end within a single entity;
  4. context-rich insights are essential for better, faster decisions.

The task is to master all of these, not sequentially but simultaneously, and to be prepared for the ones you cannot yet see.

Why healthcare providers must keep adjusting

Across markets, the economics of care are shifting as costs rise and reimbursement models evolve, forcing providers to find new efficiencies and transparency without compromising care quality. At the same time, many clinical and administrative teams are stretched thin. Reducing and automating low-value work and streamlining processes has become essential to return time to care and improve staff experience. Patients increasingly move across networks and settings; their journeys do not begin or end within a single entity, and they expect their information to follow them securely and seamlessly. Consolidation and the formation of clinical networks add further complexity, as hospital groups must operate multi-site, multi-vendor landscapes without locking in. Regulators meanwhile demand stronger privacy, security, and auditability. In addition, providers are expected to contribute to research and innovation, which requires governed, high-quality data that can be shared responsibly. In this context, change is not a project with an end date - it is a continuous capability.

Why modular, data-driven technology wins

The technological response to these realities is clear: Monoliths are giving way to composable, cloud-based platforms where capabilities can evolve independently and safely. Interoperability is a first principle: open standards make it feasible to plug and play with electronic medical record and revenue cycle systems, devices, and third-party solutions. A data-fabric architecture becomes the backbone, unifying, governing, and productizing data across silos for operations, analytics, and research. AI then moves from hype to impact when it is responsibly embedded in processes, grounded in high-quality, context-rich, governed data and managed with explainability and controls. Security and sovereignty must be designed from the beginning to keep sensitive health data under the right jurisdiction, up to and including deployment on a sovereign cloud in and for a specific country. Because healthcare runs in mixed environments, the architecture must be hybrid by design, bridging on-premises and cloud systems while preserving control. Extensibility also matters: Open APIs and both no-code, low-code, and pro-code tools featured by AI coding toolchains allow organizations to adapt quickly to new regulations, clinical workflows, and innovations without compromising the clean core that keeps your systems stable over time and allows future innovations.

How SAP contributes: a digital health platform you can compose

We at SAP recognize this and, grounded in realities at customer sites, have defined our SAP global healthcare industry strategy accordingly: To provide our current and future customers with a hybrid, modular healthcare system landscape that runs intelligent and automated processes on SAP together with solutions from our partner ecosystem, so providers can deliver better outcomes for every patient, every time.

Interoperability in an open ecosystem environment is foundational: We embrace open healthcare standards, including HL7 FHIR® through SAP Health Data Services for FHIR on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), so providers can integrate clinical and operational landscapes at scale. We are open to AnyEMR and MultiEMR, meaning you can choose the electronic medical record (EMR) systems that fit your needs, even operate multiple EMRs in parallel across a hospital group. The same openness applies to revenue cycle management (RCM): AnyRCM and MultiRCM are supported, so you can run the billing and patient-accounting systems that work best for your context and locale. While SAP does not develop or market Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) or EMR/RCM systems by ourselves, we rely on a broad partner ecosystem to extend clinical capabilities, giving customers freedom of choice and accelerating innovation. You can explore healthcare extensions on SAP Store, track progress here via SAP Healthcare Community updates and SAP News, and follow co-innovation signals through Sapphire Ventures.

A clean, modern core for business operations remains essential: SAP Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public or private edition) provides best-in-class capabilities for finance, spend management, and supply chain, forming the backbone of efficient, resilient hospital operations. You can compose end-to-end solutions by combining these capabilities with industry-specific partner solutions for hospital and clinical needs (for example, Hospital Information Systems (HIS) and Clinical Information Systems (CIS)). SAP is also verticalizing core business capabilities for healthcare and publishing industry roadmaps to provide transparency and alignment with regulatory and clinical requirements.

Data is the connective tissue: SAP Business Data Cloud implements a healthcare-ready data fabric, enabling you to access, combine, and govern context-rich data across applications and vendors with lineage and policy enforcement. It is being expanded with healthcare-specific semantics, interoperability accelerators, and governance templates for consent, retention, and de-identification, so providers can package data safely and at speed. This is how you move from siloed data to “Patient 360", blending clinical, operational, and financial signals to coordinate care, optimize throughput, and improve outcomes. It is also how you provide research-ready data products with the required quality, confidentiality, and anonymization for internal and external studies, within and across institutions, supporting the future data economy.

For a deeper dive on treating clinical and operational data as a governed organizational asset and what that implies for ownership, quality, stewardship, and value realization, see Data as medicine’s backbone: redefining its value to foster innovation in the data economy | BMJ Health & Care Informatics

AI then becomes meaningful: It begins with embedded intelligence and productive assistance, and culminates in agentic capabilities: SAP Business AI infuses intelligence across the entire SAP solution portfolio, while SAP Joule, our extensible assistant, empowers teams work smarter with enterprise context at their fingertips. With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can create governed, customer-specific AI scenarios that leverage SAP-provided access to leading foundation models, grounded in your data and subject to robust controls for safety, privacy, and auditability. Together, these layers establish the foundation on which the next evolution - agentic AI - can operate autonomously and responsibly.

All of this is orchestrated on SAP BTP, which provides services for application, process, and data integrations, APIs and eventing, including prebuild integration content, workflow, security, and both low-code/no-code and pro-code tools, all embedded in AI, you need to extend, automate, integrate, and innovate without compromising the clean core of your systems. Because sovereignty matters, you can deploy on sovereign-cloud options to meet strict residency, control, and regulatory requirements. Efficient operations and sustained transformation are anchored by SAP Services & Support and SAP Learning, complemented by experienced partners who bring proven healthcare accelerators.

Modernize at your pace

Every organization is at a different starting point. For providers adopting SAP Cloud ERP for the first time, SAP GROW accelerates deployment with best practices and fast time-to-value. For those transitioning from legacy ERP, RISE with SAP provides a guided path to SAP Cloud ERP with transformation services, tailored to guide organizations through every phase of their digital transformation journey. Many hospitals still run SAP Business Suite 7 (ECC), whose mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. To provide runway, SAP offers extended maintenance through 2030 and a Transition Option in SAP Private Cloud through 2033, so you can plan and execute your migration deliberately and de-risked. Here, most providers favor separating ERP and HIS transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data and structures and reduce risk when integrating clinical systems.

What does this mean for you, our customers

For leadership teams

Standardize the core while keeping the edge flexible. Harmonize finance, procurement, and supply chain on a clean core, and integrate AnyEMR/AnyRCM to reflect clinical realities at each site. The result is greater transparency into cost drivers, improved resilience in sourcing and inventory, and a lower total cost of ownership as cloud operations and a clean core reduce technical debt and simplify upgrades.

For clinicians and care teams

Interoperable platforms reduce friction. When the right data flows to the point of care through standards-based integration, documentation becomes lighter, handoffs become safer, and decisions are supported by timely, context-rich insights.  AI becomes a practical and personal assistant rather than a novelty, helping prepare notes, highlight risks, predict discharge readiness where appropriate, and increasingly supporting activities autonomously - always grounded in governed data. This relieves staff from administrative burden and frees up time for higher‑value, patient‑focused work.

For research and innovation leaders

A governed data fabric creates a trustworthy pipeline for studies and collaborations. You can publish well-defined data products with privacy and provenance protected by policy, enabling partnerships with universities, life sciences, and public health without reinventing the wheel each time.

For hospital groups and networks

Open, modular architectures enable multi-site agility. Organizations can operate MultiEMR/MultiRCM across entities, harmonize where it counts, and maintain local autonomy where needed, avoiding lock-in while still gaining economies of scale.

For IT, security, and compliance teams

Unified governance brings consistency. Policies apply across applications and data, controls are auditable, and residency requirements can be met with sovereign-cloud deployments when needed.

These are not theoretical benefits: Hirslanden, for example, modernized core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio solutions, an illustration of how process intelligence can accelerate transformation in healthcare. Similar quick wins are within reach: streamline patient flow and bed capacity with a single view of clinical and operational signals; optimize pharmacy and inventory with demand forecasting and automated replenishment; strengthen revenue integrity and coding with governed clinical and billing data; provision research datasets with automated de-identification and access controls; and boost workforce productivity through embedded analytics and assistants such as SAP Joule.

A call to action: A phased path to your digital health platform

The destination is not a single product: It is an operating model which supports ongoing transformations. A practical path begins with alignment on priorities: map your landscape, pain points, and the three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. Define your guiding principles and develop your target modular enterprise architecture accordingly. Start with the data fabric by setting up SAP Business Data Cloud and publishing your first data products, such as Patient 360 and supply-chain performance, with clear governance and policies.  In parallel, you can modernize the core - either by starting fresh with the GROW with SAP journey, designed to consume the entire SAP Business Suite from the public cloud with best practices and continuous innovation, and rapid time‑to‑value or by modernizing your existing landscape with the RISE with SAP journey, typically moving to SAP Cloud ERP in a private‑cloud model combined with specialized public‑cloud LoB solutions. This hybrid path supports a clean‑core roadmap, harmonized processes, and a transformation pace aligned to your needs. Integrate for interoperability by deploying SAP Integration Suite and SAP Health Data Services for FHIR and connecting AnyEMR/AnyRCM and priority partner solutions from SAP partner ecosystem for healthcare industry. Prove value quickly and already during your transformation by launching two time-boxed pilots: one operational, one clinical-adjacent with measurable KPIs and executive sponsorship. Then scale responsibly: expand analytics and AI with governance-by-design and deploy in line with residency and sovereignty requirements.

This is how we aim to help so that your healthcare organizations run better and deliver high-quality, outcomes-based care together with our partners.

entry.title: Bank Statement Reconciliation
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am performing the bank reconciliation of a vendor payment that had already been posted in the system, using the “Manage Bank Statements” app. When I reconcile it with the corresponding line in the bank statement, I notice that the clearing account shows an open item both in positive and negative amounts.

I also expected that, when reconciling the payment with the bank statement line, the item in the bank clearing account would be automatically cleared, but this is not happening. I would like to understand why this behavior occurs.

My specific questions are:

  • Why does the item in the clearing account not get automatically cleared when the payment is matched with the bank statement?

  • Is it normal for the clearing account to generate two open items (one debit and one credit) after reconciling a payment that was already posted?

  • Should the automatic clearing of the payment with the invoice be executed before or after the bank reconciliation?

  • Does automatic clearing always need to be performed, even when bank reconciliation is done in Bank Statement Processing?

I would appreciate any guidance or experience regarding the correct process flow.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Outbound iDocs are not randomly not generated
entry.content:
1. Analyze the Failed Cases
Since replication isn’t possible, focus on the six historical incidents. Compare the warehouse tasks that did generate iDocs vs. those that did not. Look for subtle differences for storage type, bin type, handling unit assignment, batch status, or special stock indicators. Check if the materials involved had any unusual master data settings (e.g., missing output determination key fields).

2. System Logs & Monitoring

  • SM58 (Transactional RFC): See if any outbound LUWs were stuck or failed at the time.
  • SMQ1/SMQ2 (Queues): Check if the outbound queue had errors.
  • WE02/WE05 (iDoc monitoring): Confirm whether the iDoc was never created or created but failed to send.
  • SLG1 (Application logs): Look for error messages tied to warehouse task confirmation.

3. Potential Root Causes

  • Race conditions: If multiple tasks are confirmed simultaneously, the output determination might skip one.
  • Custom code: If there’s a user exit/BAdI filtering which tasks should trigger iDocs, it may be excluding certain cases unintentionally.
  • Authorization issues: Rarely, if the user confirming the task lacks authorization for the output type, the iDoc won’t be generated.
  • Update termination: If the update task failed silently, the material movement would post but the iDoc creation routine wouldn’t complete.

4. Preventive Measures
Implement additional logging in the iDoc creation user exit/BAdI to capture why an iDoc wasn’t generated. Set up alerts for when a warehouse task is confirmed without a corresponding iDoc (cross‑check tables). If possible, create a reconciliation report comparing SAP bin status vs. MES bin status daily to catch discrepancies early.

entry.title: Re: Qualification in Routing Operations S4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @kylianswinkels98 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, there is no CBC configuration that lets you maintain a free “list of qualifications” for CA0x routing directly, which is why you cannot find it in CBC or the workforce apps. In the CA01/CA02/CA03, the fields Job/Position/Qualification come from the classic “personnel qualification requirement” concept. In practice, these fields only become fully usable when the system has an underlying qualification catalog and the related integration, which is traditionally HCM or a PEO based qualification setup.

If your tenant uses PEO and supports qualification handling, you can handle qualification via Manage Object Qualification Assignments. This is where qualification is used in the manufacturing context, instead of maintaining a custom list in CA0x.  

Suggested best practice is:

  • Not to create a ZZ field just to replicate GL account or qualification style logic unless you have a clear extensibility requirement.
  • For specific case, the clean approach is:
    • For you to use standard qualification assignment apps, or
    • If not available, do not rely on the qualification fields in CA01/CA02/CA03 because you cannot effectively maintain them in standard Public Cloud.

If your PEO apps exist but CA0x value help is not consistent, i suggest you raise an incident because it may be a UI or scope issue. Else, it is a feature gap which you can submit a improvement request rather than a CBC confit switch.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: What's New in Supply Chain in SAP Cloud ERP 2602?
entry.content:
In this blog, we're excited to introduce you to some of the most impactful updates for Supply Chain in the Cloud ERP 2602 release. This update is all about empowering you with greater visibility, streamlined processes, and enhanced flexibility across your entire supply chain. Whether you work in inventory, warehousing, or transportation, these new features are designed to make your daily work more efficient and intelligent. Below, you'll find a summary of the main highlights from each area of our supply chain solution, and if you follow the links, you'll find a comprehensive overview of further new features in each area from our expert product managers.


2602 Release Highlights Overview

To kick things off, we've put together an overview of our top  supply chain highlights in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP.

 

Warehouse Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

One fresh Warehouse Management feature we're keen to highlight is the new Joule capability to assist with your Warehouse Management Operations, which we'll look at below. Of course, there's much more to come -  make sure to check out @Christian_Ries blog for the full scope of release highlights in Warehouse Management in the 2602 release. 

Joule Enablement For Outbound Delivery Orders & Warehouse Tasks

Building on recent enhancements, we continue to expand the capabilities of Joule, our generative AI assistant. Now, Joule can assist users in accessing and managing key business objects in Warehouse Management using natural language. You can use simple conversational language to process delivery orders and warehouse tasks, ask questions, display data, and receive navigation links to relevant applications. Joule can also perform actions such as creating and updating business objects. This functionality simplifies user interaction and empowers your team to control warehouse operations with greater efficiency and accuracy, directly through a natural language interface.

 

Automatic Packing for Direct Goods Receipt Posting after Production Order Confirmation 

A recent key enhancement automates the packing of goods following production. Previously, a direct goods receipt could be posted automatically upon confirmation of a production order. Now, this process has been enhanced to include automatic packing. The system can be configured to automatically pack any unpacked products into handling units based on predefined packaging instructions. This action is triggered during the confirmation of a manufacturing order, eliminating the need for manual user input. This feature increases efficiency on the production floor by combining automatable steps and improves accuracy by providing total automation from confirmation to packed stock, which reduces the potential for human error.

 

Transportation Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

In the below demo, @Markus_Oertelt talks you through the complete transportation lifecycle associated with subcontracting purchase orders. This seamless integration is released in 2602 and enables transportation planning, execution and freight settlement via subcontractors with minimal manual effort and reduced scope for errors. Of course, that's not all, and for an overview of further TM highlights in this release, check this blog out from TM Expert @thomas_roesch.

 

Enable Subcontracting Purchase Order Integration With Transportation Management

This powerful new feature enables seamless integration of subcontracting purchase orders with Transportation Management (TM). Users can now manage the complete transportation lifecycle associated with the subcontracting process, from planning transportation demand based on purchase order requirements to executing movements and settling freight costs for both outbound and inbound processes. For outbound processes, transportation demand from the delivery to the subcontractor can be planned, executed, and settled. For inbound processes, the same applies to the delivery of the finished product. This integration streamlines the end-to-end process, reduces manual effort, ensures accurate cost allocation, and provides real-time visibility, allowing for greater control and transparency over subcontracting logistics.

 


Core Logistics in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

Under core logistics, we include the backbone of any well-oiled supply chain solution, featuring inventory management, delivery management, handling-unit & serial number management. With release 2602, we're particularly excited by the improved user experience when managing physical inventory delivered by the new app Manage Physical Inventory Items List. Of course, that's not all, and for some more core logistics highlights, look no further than the overview blog by @Greg_Hutcheon1. 

Introducing Manage Physical Inventory Item List (App F8634)


In the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP, SAP introduces the new Fiori app Manage Physical Inventory Item List (F8634) to help inventory managers process physical inventory faster and with greater accuracy. Instead of switching between multiple documents and apps, users get a single, structured list report with clear status visibility across all relevant physical inventory items, making it easy to see what’s pending, in progress, or complete.

The app offers flexible filters (for example by plant, storage location, planned count date, and processing status) so managers can quickly focus on the right subset of items—such as “Counted” entries ready for follow-up. From the same screen, they can execute key actions like posting, triggering recounts, or printing, including mass processing across multiple inventory documents. In a typical flow, managers review counted items, identify differences, post items with no variance in one step, and initiate recounts for major discrepancies by setting a recount date and generating a new physical inventory document. If configured, the app also supports compliant processing by prompting for reason codes during posting. Overall, F8634 improves transparency, reduces errors, and streamlines inventory processing from count to resolution.

 


Order Promising

New BAdI for Confirmation Acceptance Rule

In this release, SAP Cloud ERP added a powerful new extension point for Order Promising: the Confirmation Adjustment (Confirmation Acceptance) BAdI, also known as “adjust ATP confirmation.” It lets you take the standard confirmations calculated by SAP ATP—quantities and dates—then apply your own business logic on top after the basic ATP check.

With this BAdI, you can override confirmed quantities or shift confirmation dates to better match business priorities (like customer tiers, allocation rules, or operational constraints). The adjusted confirmation can be written back and persisted in the relevant tables, so downstream processes work with the business-approved result—not just the original ATP output.

To keep things safe and explainable, SAP includes validations to prevent over-confirmation, and the system records the custom outcome in the ATP check log for full transparency. The adjusted values can also influence BOP decisions(for example via FIXMG), giving you more control over how confirmations behave during backorder processing.

Supply Protection Indicator in the Display Product Availability App

The Display Product Availability app now includes an easy-to-understand Supply Protection (SUP) indicator with two states (on/off). It adds immediate context to the available quantity by showing whether that quantity may be subject to supply protection.

Note: The icon is shown as active when supply protection is enabled for the specific product in the specific plant.

A SUP icon now appears directly next to a product’s available quantity. It indicates whether Supply Protection is considered in the availability calculation, helping users quickly interpret what they’re seeing without extra checks. This improves day-to-day decision-making by giving users instant visibility into whether the displayed quantity is “straight availability” or potentially restricted by supply protection—saving time and reducing confusion during availability checks.

 

Summary

This blog provides an overview of the key features and functions delivered in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP. Make sure to follow the links above to find about more supply chain features in depth with the relevant specialist blogs. Beyond supply chain capabilities, the 2602 SAP Cloud ERP release was packed full of great new features designed to drive success. For an overview of the key highlights from this release, make sure to check out the 2602 release blog by @BeSchulze  

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Click on the enablement wheel below and check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you:

entry.title: Re: A class already exists with the name "LCL_CUSTOM_HEADER"
entry.content:
EDIT 2026-02-20: I'm copying this from the only comment (in 2024), assuming that it has solved the issue.

It's possible that you have moved your local class from one include to another, and you are activating only one include. Activate all the includes at the same time. It's also possible that you have 2 INCLUDE statements of the same include program, or two declarations CLASS lcl_custom_header, in that case fix your code.

I used getResultMember() instead of getResultSet() — I was trying to get a single member, but getResultSet() is the correct way to get the full row data including properties. I tried to access properties directly as member.properties["ExtLink"] — The correct path is deeper: member[0]["Story"].properties["Story.ExtLink"]. I missed both the array index [0], the dimension name ["Story"], and the fact that the property key needs the dimension prefix "Story.ExtLink" not just "ExtLink".

entry.title: SAP Data Reconciliation: Moving from Matching Maze to The Autonomous Future
entry.content:
BACKGROUND

There are two types of SAP reconciliation meetings during cutover.

Meeting Type 1 (What we planned):

“Let’s quickly confirm data moved correctly.”

Meeting Type 2 (What actually happens):

  • Finance says revenue looks off.
  • Procurement says suppliers are complaining.
  • Supply chain says inventory is inflated.
  • IT says record counts match.
  • Everyone stares at IT.

Welcome to the beautiful chaos of SAP data reconciliation.

Whether you’re:

  • Splitting a company
  • Running S/4 alongside ECC
  • Sharing data with subsidiaries
  • Migrating to a new instance
  • Or integrating systems after M&A

Data matching in SAP is not just technical data reconciliation. It’s business continuity surgery.

Why SAP Reconciliation Feels So Painful Today with current ETL tools?

Let’s be honest about the current state.

We Reconcile Tables, Not Decisions

We check:

  • Counts
  • Totals
  • Field matches

But business runs on:

  • Commitments
  • Revenue recognition
  • Inventory valuation
  • Working capital

Matching tables ≠ preserving business intent.

SAP Processes Are Chains, Not Records

Every transaction is part of a flow:

  • Forecast → Production → Inventory → Sale → Revenue
  • Contract → Order → Delivery → Billing → Cash
  • Budget → Commitment → Accrual → Payment

Break one link, and financial truth bends quietly.

Reconciliation today doesn’t understand chains. It understands spreadsheets.

Customization Is Everywhere

Every SAP system has:

  • Custom fields
  • Custom pricing logic
  • Custom movement types
  • Custom account determination
  • Custom approval flows

Generic reconciliation tools look at this and say: “Good luck.”

Real Business Reconciliation KPIs (Not Row Counts)

Let’s talk about reconciliation the way your CFO thinks about it.

Procure-to-Pay (P2P): The “Where Did My Money Go?” Flow

Business Flow:

PO Amount → GR Amount → Invoice Amount → Payment Amount

Real Business KPIs:

PO to GR Consumption Ratio

What % of PO value has been physically received?

  • Detects over-delivery or under-delivery
  • Identifies ghost POs
  • Highlights operational leakage
If PO is $1M and GR is $1.4M, someone is shopping.

GR to Invoice Variance %

Are vendors invoicing more than what was received?

  • Detects pricing issues
  • Catches duplicate invoicing
  • Flags freight/tax mismatches

This is not technical reconciliation. This is margin protection.

Order-to-Cash (O2C): The “Where Did My Revenue Go?” Flow

Business Flow:

Sales Order → Delivery → Billing → AR → Cash

Real Business KPIs:

Order Fulfillment Integrity

% of orders fully delivered as priced.

  • Detects partial deliveries not billed
  • Finds pricing condition errors
  • Highlights logistics-billing gaps

Delivery-to-Billing Variance

Does billing reflect delivered quantity and agreed price? This catches:

  • Wrong pricing conditions
  • Missed surcharges
  • Freight miscalculation

Revenue leakage hides here.

Manufacturing: The “Why Is My Warehouse Lying?” Flow

Business Flow:

Material → Production → Inventory → Valuation → COGS

Real Business KPIs:

Inventory Valuation Consistency

Does total inventory value match between systems by:

  • Plant
  • Material group
  • Valuation class

Mismatch here = financial statement distortion.

BOM Integrity Impact

% of production orders where:

  • Component consumption matches BOM
  • Standard cost matches actual cost

Incorrect BOM migration? It is cost explosion.

The Real Question for the Future

When we reshare data between SAP systems in the future, we should not ask:

“Did the data copy and tables and fields match?”

We should ask:

“Did the business outcome stay intact?”

That is a completely different problem. And it requires a completely different capability set.

The Future: Business-First, Agentic SAP Reconciliation

Let’s imagine what reconciliation should look like 5 years from now.

Not reactive. Not 400 dashboards..

But autonomous, business-aware, and proactive.

Flow-Centric Reconciliation

Instead of reconciling:

  • Material master
  • Vendor master
  • GL master

We reconcile:

  • Revenue lifecycle integrity
  • Working capital stability
  • Margin preservation
  • Inventory flow accuracy
  • Commitment-to-cash cycles

Example:

Instead of: “Material record count and fields matches.” The system says:

“Inventory valuation variance of 1.8% driven by missing moving average price adjustments in 3 plants. Financial impact: $2.1M.”

That’s business reconciliation.

Agentic Automation Capabilities of Future Data Reconciliation Tool

Here’s what real agentic SAP reconciliation must be capable of:

1. Business Context Modeling

The system must understand:

  • How revenue is recognized
  • How inventory is valued
  • How pricing conditions affect margin
  • How tax determination impacts compliance
  • How cost flows through production

Not just tables — but intent.

2. Cross-Process Correlation

If:

  • Billing increases
  • But cash conversion drops
  • And AR aging shifts

The system correlates these across modules. SD + FI + CO + Treasury. Today, that takes 3 teams and 2 weeks.

Future system: “Cash conversion delay traced to payment term misalignment for customers migrated from legacy region.”

3. KPI Stability Monitoring

When data is migration between SAP systems, the system should monitor:

  • Revenue per customer
  • Margin per product
  • Inventory turns
  • Working capital ratio
  • DSO / DPO shifts
  • Production yield

If KPIs deviate outside normal behavior bands. Alert triggered. Because business drift matters more than table drift.

4. Intelligent Variance Classification

Not every difference is a problem. Agentic reconciliation should classify:

  • Configuration difference
  • Timing difference
  • Master data gap
  • Transaction sequencing issue
  • True financial error

And suggest action.

Not just: “Mismatch found.”
But: “Difference due to delayed goods issue posting in target system. Expected to auto-correct in next batch.”

That reduces panic and so many meetings.

Executive-Ready Narrative

Future reconciliation shouldn’t produce:

  • 47 dashboards
  • 16 pivot graphs
  • A nervous data analyst

It should produce:

“Post SAP data migration, revenue and margin remain stable within historical variance bands. Minor inventory timing differences detected in 2 plants with no financial impact. No systemic control breaks identified.”

That’s board ready confidence.

The Big Shift: From Data Movement to Business Assurance

Old model:

Move data. Validate data. Sign off.

Future model:

Simulate business impact. Continuously assure process integrity. Autonomously detect drift. Explain root cause.

Reconciliation becomes less about matching numbers…and more about protecting enterprise behavior.

What Organizations Expect in Future SAP Reconciliations?

In the future, companies won’t ask: “How many records mismatched?” They’ll ask:

  • Are warehouse movements as expected?
  • Did margin integrity hold?
  • Did working capital spike?
  • Did revenue timing shift?
  • Did operational velocity slow down?
  • Did risk exposure increase?
  • Does financial demand and supply are working as expected?

That’s reconciliation redefined in the era of agentic automation!!

entry.content:
I am running into an issue with the "My Processes" App in a shared environment.

When I share an environment with someone (with the ProcessAutomationParticipant role collection) and don't give them the Supervise authorization they are not able to see the processes they are involved in in that shared environment, however when I give them the Supervise authorization then they are able to see all of the processes from that environment in the "My Processes" app. Is that intended? Is there a way to give someone access to a shared environment in a way that they are able to only see the processes they are directly involved in? The "My Requests" App seems to work fine in that regard since it doesn't require the Supervise authorization but only the Execute authorization.

entry.title: Re: Upgrade SAP S/4HANA from 2021 to 2023 FPS02: Wrong Fi...
entry.content:
Good Afternoon,

Thank you for your follow-up, even more than a year later 😊

As mentioned in my original post, the issue is not related to SUM.

The root cause lies within Maintenance Planner.
Maintenance Planner generated a stack that does not comply with SAP’s own recommendations because it selected a lower Support Package (SP) level for SAP Fiori Front-End Server for SAP S/4HANA 2023 than for the other SAP Fiori components.
This inconsistency in SP levels led to the issue.

Regards,

entry.title: Re: Adaptation project: Override not being called/not wor...
entry.content:
The reason that changes are not applied when running on the ABAP system could be that the app variant is not started at all but the original app is started instead. This could be caused by incorrect target mappings, which maybe do not fit or are ambiguous.

The best way to check this is to check the network trace when starting the app. Here look for a file called manifest.appdescr_variant. Open its content and under sap.app/id, you will find what is really started.
If it does not match your project Id, your target mappings do not work.

entry.title: How Do VARs Continue to Offer L1/L2 Support After Migrating to SAP Cloud ALM?
entry.content:
How do VARs continue to offer L1/L2 support to end customers after migrating to SAP Cloud ALM? And how can tickets be escalated to SAP if needed?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How Do VARs Continue to Offer L1/L2 Support After Mig...
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ALM does not include an IT Service Desk. If VARs wish to continue with L1/L2 ticket support, External API Management can be used to connect an SAP Cloud ALM tenant to any 3rd party service desk solutions.

That 3rd party ticketing tool can then use the SAP Cloud ALM ITSM API to forward those tickets to the SAP Support Backbone. The goal of the SAP Cloud ALM ITSM API is to support cases management from your own ITSM system. This API lets you integrate SAP cases manages in your incident and problem processes. Cases created/managed via the SAP Cloud ALM ITSM API are fully compatible with SAP standard support access points, i.e. SAP for Me, SAP for Me mobile app, Built-In Support.

3565170 - VAR case forwarding via SAP Cloud ALM ITSM API

Other similar questions and their answers regarding the transition from SAP Solution Manager to SAP Cloud ALM can be found here. For more information on Transitioning to SAP Cloud ALM, check the linked web page.

entry.title: Re: mm02 attachments in smartforms
entry.content:
Your code is about reading the documents in MM02 (SAP component CA-DMS), but maybe the OP is talking about GOS attachments.

Also, you're only returning the image as XSTRING but for Smart Forms it's completely different you must create the image in SE78 dynamically. How to upload images in SE78 has been answered in older questions.

NB: for information, your code:

        "Get file data
        OPEN DATASET lv_file FOR INPUT IN BINARY MODE.
        IF sy-subrc = 0.
          DO.                                            "#EC CI_NESTED
            READ DATASET lv_file INTO ls_line.
            IF sy-subrc = 0.
              APPEND ls_line TO lt_file_data.
            ELSE.
              APPEND ls_line TO lt_file_data.
              EXIT.
            ENDIF.
          ENDDO.
          CLOSE DATASET lv_file.
        ENDIF.

can be simplified to one read because because the file is opened with IN BINARY MODE and EV_STREAM is of type XSTRING, so this one READ will read the whole file:

        "Get file data
        OPEN DATASET lv_file FOR INPUT IN BINARY MODE.
        IF sy-subrc = 0.
          READ DATASET lv_file INTO ev_stream. "#EC CI_SUBRC
          CLOSE DATASET lv_file.
        ENDIF.

entry.title: 07.SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation - Top 10 Project Execution Risks and Mitigation Plan
entry.content:
This is a continuous blog series on the SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation. This blog explores the most common execution risks I have observed in SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementations and outlines practical mitigation strategies based on my experience.

Over the course of my career, I have been involved in more than 40 SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation projects, serving roles ranging from consultant to architect, owning project deliverables, and working with partners to provide advisory guidance. Each project journey has been different, bringing unique challenges, cultural nuances, system landscapes, complex requirements, heavily customized processes, and business expectations.

While many assume that implementation risks primarily stem from product gaps or technical limitations, my experience tells a different story. In the vast majority of cases, project implementation challenges arise not from system limitations, but from human factors(errors) — decision delays, lack of skill, unclear ownership, communication gaps, misaligned expectations, or inadequate governance.

 

Every SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation begins with a strong intent:

 To eliminate legacy system challenges

  • To introduce a modern, sophisticated HCM platform
  • To standardize and streamline HR processes
  • To deliver within agreed timelines
  • To stay within the approved budget
  • Deliver high customer satisfaction and adoption

Yet despite clear objectives and executive sponsorship, some projects often face execution risks that threaten timelines, budgets, and stakeholder confidence.

 

Risk 1) Strategy and Plan in Place but No Tangible Outcomes Delivered :

 Risk Description: Although a clear strategy and a detailed plan were defined, and iterations were planned and executed accordingly, the expected outcomes were not achieved.

The implementation phase progressed smoothly up to System Integration Testing (SIT). However, during SIT and User Acceptance Testing (UAT), significant gaps were identified. These included newly emerging requirements, unresolved data quality issues, and incomplete system integrations. As a result, the project experienced delays, rework, and misalignment with expected deliverables.

“Creating a strategy is not complex, but execution and delivering the outcomes are the biggest challenges.”

Mitigation Plan:

This is a common risk in complex projects. To mitigate it:

  1. Define clear, measurable success criteria and expected outcomes for each project phase.
  2. Thoroughly validate requirements before moving into the build and testing phases.
  3. Every stage of the project must be evaluated based on measurable outcomes and value delivered — not merely on task completion or milestone achievement.
  4. Conduct early data profiling and integration validation to identify issues upfront.
  5. Introduce interim checkpoints focused on business outcomes rather than just task completion.
  6. Ensure stakeholder sign-off at each stage based on deliverable quality and readiness.
  7. Success Criteria for each Iteration, migration mock load, testing, integration build, etc.

Risk 2 ) Unskilled Key Resources from the System Integrator (SI):

Risk Description:  The customer selected SI to implement SAP SuccessFactors HCM following a thorough RFP process and validation. However, SI’s resources lack the skills, leading to major quality issues and failing to meet expected standards or deliver on promises. As a result, project quality declines and stakeholder confidence erodes.

"In my experience, this is one of the most critical risks in any SAP transformation program".

Mitigation Plan:

 Customers must proactively evaluate and validate the competency of the SI’s key resources before and during the project. The most critical roles to assess include: 

 

  • Project Manager/Delivery
  • Solution Architect
  • Module Leads
  • Technical Architect
  • Data Migration Lead

 

These are strategic SI roles that directly determine the success of the implementation. As a customer, you are entitled to demand the right skill set and experience level aligned with your transformation goals.

Example Context: SAP S/4HANA HCM to SAP SuccessFactors Transformation:

 For organizations transitioning from SAP S/4HANA HCM to SAP SuccessFactors HCM, cross-platform expertise is essential. The SI team must demonstrate proven experience in both on-premise HCM environments and cloud-based SuccessFactors architecture to ensure seamless integration, data integrity, and process continuity

Required Competency Profile for Key SI Roles

Risk 3) Resource Availability and Not Taking Ownership of Project Execution by the Customer

Risk Description: The notion that a System Integrator (SI) should fully drive the project and ensure successful delivery is a myth. While the SI plays a very critical role, the ultimate accountability for project success lies with the customer. Without strong customer ownership, project oversight often faces delays, scope creep, misalignment with business objectives, and poor adoption.

 

Mitigation Plan:

 Customers must take active ownership of project delivery by owning each work stream and customer leads take key roles and assign dedicated resouces:

  • Establishing a strong internal project sponsor and governance structure
  • Defining clear business objectives and success criteria
  • Allocating dedicated business and technical stakeholders
  • Making timely decisions and resolving escalations
  • Actively participating in requirement validation, testing, and change management
  • Monitoring progress through structured reviews and performance metrics
  • Prior to project initiation, confirm resource planning and allocation from the customer’s side.
  • Identify key business, technical, and business analyst resources, ensuring they are dedicated and available to work with the System Integrator (SI) throughout the project.
  • Customer Leads must own the deliverables and ensure that SI delivers them.
  • Create a detailed RACI and identify customer deliverables

For more detailed guidance, refer to the blog mentioned below.

https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/03-sap-successfactors-hcm-implementation-phase-0-customer-readiness-and/ba-p/14292649

Risk 4) Never-Ending Scope and Requirements (Scope Creep)

Risk Description: Scope creep is one of the biggest risk factors in project delivery. While the System Integrator (SI) may define the scope and set aggressive timelines at the beginning, continuous change requests during implementation can significantly extend delivery timelines, increase costs, and impact overall project quality.

Frequent change orders often become part of the implementation process, adding complexity and delaying milestones.

Mitigation Plan:

  • Conduct a thorough Phase 0 (Discovery/Planning Phase) to clearly define and validate requirements, and validate the scope defined by SI.
  • As a customer, review and analyze the scope multiple times to ensure it is accurately and comprehensively documented in the Statement of Work (SOW).
  • Establish strong governance and a formal Change Control Process.
  • Implement a structured approval mechanism for all change requests, including impact analysis (cost, timeline, resources, and go-live impact) before approval.
  • Maintain clear documentation and traceability of all scope changes.

Risk 5) Overselling and Aggressive Timelines by System Integrator (SI) :

 Risk Description: The consulting market is highly competitive, and overselling is a common practice to win deals. But this can lead to unrealistic expectations, project delays, increased costs, and failure to achieve expected outcomes.

Mitigation Plan:

  1. Validate proposed timelines and ensure they are realistic.
  2. Leverage your knowledge of your business processes, customizations, complexity, testing requirements, data, integrations, and organizational culture to assess feasibility.
  3. If timelines appear unrealistic, plan for an adequate buffer time, a minimum 20% extra time/Budget.
  4. Challenge the SI and request realistic data points to support the proposed schedule.

Risk 6) Treating the Project as a Technical Upgrade - No Transformation 

Risk Description: Approaching the project as merely a technical product update—such as replicating the legacy system on a different tech stack—overlooks the transformation potential of every SAP SuccessFactors implementation. This can result in missed opportunities to redefine processes and improve business outcomes. This will lead to not reaching business outcomes.

Mitigation Plan:

  1. Avoid treating the project purely as a technical upgrade; ensure business takes considerations drive key decisions.
  2. Maintain a balance between the business and technical teams,
    • Business team: Owns process redesign and defines requirements.
    • Technical team: Validates SI deliverables and ensures technical feasibility.
  3. Emphasize collaboration between business and tech teams throughout the implementation to prevent misalignment.

Risk 7)Underestimating the integration effort

 Risk Description: Integration efforts require substantial coordination, technical design, and joint ownership between the Customer and System Integrator (SI). Historically, integration scope and complexity are underestimated, and development is initiated too late in the delivery lifecycle.

 

When the integration work stream is not executed correctly, the project poses a high risk to the overall program's success.

Mitigation Plan:

 

  • Establish a comprehensive inventory of all inbound and outbound integrations at project initiation.
  • Classify each interface with a clear disposition strategy: Retain, Redesign, Retire, or Replace.
  • Confirm architecture principles and integration standards upfront.
  • Secure formal sign-off on the integration scope baseline.
  • Sequence integrations based on Business criticality, Technical complexity, and dependency on core object configuration.
  • Finalize the EC data model during Iteration 1 to 80-90% of fields. This enables early interface design , mapping and build
  • Initiate build activities for high-risk integrations as early as possible.
  • Integration success requires active customer engagement. The customer must assign committed resources .
  • Each interface must have a completed Testing Evidence Document.
  • Review the testing results and provide sign-off before moving to SIT.

Risk 8 ) Underestimating the Data Migration effort:

 Risk Description: Data migration is a critical success factor in any implementation project. Historically, many project delays are caused by underestimating the complexity, effort, and ownership required for data migration.

 Mitigation Plan:

 

  •  Clearly define the scope of data migration based on the customer’s current system and business requirements.
  •  Adopt a top-of-stack migration approach (migrate only necessary, current, and active data).
  • Limit historical data migration wherever possible. Load a maximum of one year of historical data unless legally or operationally required.
  • Know Your Data – Clean Before You Migrate. Perform detailed data profiling and analysis before mock loads.
  • Fix data quality issues in the source system before migration, and don’t wait to fix after Mock loads.
  • The customer must assign dedicated business and technical resources to provide requirements, own the work stream, validate data, and cleanse data.
  • Choose the right tools aligned with SAP's leading practice recommendations for data migration and validation.

Risk 9)Underestimating Testing  effort

 Risk Description: Testing takes significant effort, duration, and resources from the customer and SI.

Mitigation Plan:

 

  • Identify and document end-to-end test scenarios covering all business processes, integrations, and cross-module workflows as early as possible in the project lifecycle. This ensures complete process validation and minimizes gaps.
  • Utilize testing automation tools for regression testing and configuration validation. Automation improves efficiency, ensures repeatability, and reduces manual effort during multiple test cycles.
  • Perform detailed functional testing at the end of every iteration. Early validation of configurations and enhancements helps reduce last-minute surprises during User Acceptance Testing (UAT).
  • Validate inter-module integrations and process dependencies before executing full end-to-end testing. Ensuring that upstream and downstream processes work seamlessly reduces rework during later testing phases.
  • Allocate adequate resources for the testing workstream and provide structured training to the customer team on SAP SuccessFactors HCM. Empowering the client team ensures effective participation during System Integration Testing (SIT) and UAT.

 

Risk 10) Lack of transparency in Project status reporting and Risk identification.

Risk Description: There is a risk that project status reporting does not provide full transparency across all workstreams. Often, project reports indicate a positive status during earlier phases, but significant issues surface only at the System Integration Testing (SIT) stage.

The primary cause of this lack of transparency is insufficient workstream-level visibility and delayed risk escalation. As a customer, it is important to request and review progressively detailed updates throughout the project lifecycle to ensure early identification of potential issues.

Mitigation Plan:

  • Conduct regular status reviews for each workstream to ensure continuous visibility and alignment.
  • Track measurable outcomes, key deliverables, milestones, and cross-stream dependencies.
  • Establish clear ownership, defined roles, and accountability for each workstream.
  • Implement a structured risk management framework and maintain an active risk register, including impact assessment and mitigation tracking.
  • Promote a culture of early risk identification and escalation without penalty or blame.
  • Conduct retrospective meetings after every major milestone to evaluate performance and challenges.
  • Capture lessons learned and identify improvement opportunities.
  • Implement corrective and preventive actions to avoid recurrence of similar issues.

entry.title: Re: Contingent Worker/External to Employee Conversions th...
entry.content: This is not a viable solution for us, and likely not for others as well. Our contingent workers typically end their assignment on Friday and return on Monday as employees. We enter the contingent worker termination on Sunday to ensure they do not lose IT access during the transition to employee status. Even if we were to terminate them on Saturday, that would leave only one day for HR to initiate onboarding, for the contingent worker to complete their tasks, and for HR to finalize onboarding as a new hire. During this gap, the worker would lose access and it's not feasible to complete all tasks in one day. In Onboarding 1.0, the system recognized future‑dated terminations and allowed workers to proceed with onboarding using a future hire date without requiring termination. Why was this functionality not considered in Onboarding 2.0? What was previously an automated process has now become highly manual.

entry.title: Re: Contingent Worker/External to Employee Conversions th...
entry.content:
@tracymca 

This is not a viable solution for us, and likely not for others as well. Our contingent workers typically end their assignment on Friday and return on Monday as employees. We enter the contingent worker termination on Sunday to ensure they do not lose IT access during the transition to employee status. It is business critical they do not lose access.

Even if we were to terminate them on Saturday, that would leave only one day for HR to initiate onboarding, for the contingent worker to complete their tasks, and for HR to finalize onboarding as a new hire. During this gap, the worker would lose access and it's not feasible to complete all tasks in one day.

In Onboarding 1.0, the system recognized future‑dated terminations and allowed workers to proceed with onboarding using a future termination date as long as the hire date was after the termination date.  Why was this functionality not considered in Onboarding 2.0?

What was previously an automated process has now become highly manual.

entry.title: Why ABAP do not have JSON de/serializer in kernel?
entry.content:
And with JSON I mean 'canonical' default JSON as per JavaScript, which widely spread and supported in many languages. And not asJSON (ABAP Serialization JSON) which is supported by transformations in ABAP. 

All other de/serializations written in ABAP, like /ui2/cl_json, new one in cloud xco_json, and many open source ones are very slow, and cannot be fast as written in ABAP.

I summarized my idea in improvement request https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#idea/367772, if you think it is relevant for you too, please vote.

entry.title: Re: Delete F4579 configuration
entry.content:
Hello,
BC maintenance objects (R3TR SMBC) can be deleted like any other repository object in ADT.
Just removing the TADIR entry does not delete the object from the relevant table SMBC_CONFIG/SMBC_CONFIG_TEXT. Instead, you can't work with the object anymore. The F4579 will still show it because the relevant CDS view does not care about TADIR, only SMBC_CONFIG. I suggest you restore the TADIR entry and the delete it in ADT.
Regards,
Patrick

entry.title: Re: Central Finance Tips and Tricks #5 - SLT
entry.content:
Hi @MarcE 
I have a ongoing issue in SLT. Do you have any idea how to resolve this?

Issue: Filters (Conversion logic) not working.

Scenario:
We have 3 non-SAP Source systems from which the data is being extracted and transformed by a 3rd party ETL tool and then pushed into SLT system and the expectation is that the data would flow onto the CFIN system. However, we have only 2 environments for SLT system (Pre-PROD & PROD). So, the idea is to create 2 Mass Transfer IDs and put Filters (for both Initial Load & Ongoing Replication) to be able to segregate data from Source (DEV) & Source (QAS) and then push it to respective Target CFIN (DEV) & CFIN (QAS) systems. The filters should ensure that Source DEV data gets pushed to Target Dev and Source QAS data gets pushed to Target QAS.

Steps Followed:
1. Created 3 RFCs
A. Pointing from SLT to SLT (RFC_SLT)
B. Pointing from SLT to CFIN DEV (RFC_CFIN_D)
C. Pointing from SLT to CFIN QAS (RFC_CFIN_Q)

2. Created 2 MTID
A. MTID_SLT_CFIN_D [Used RFC_SLT in Source Settings & RFC_CFIN_D in target settings].
B. MTID_SLT_CFIN_Q [Used RFC_SLT in Source Settings & RFC_CFIN_Q in target settings].

3. Create Predefined Load / Replication Objects
A. For Load Object:
Copied from Std. Repl Object (Project: REPL_CFIN, Subproject: REPL_CFIN_ST, Predefined Object: CFIST_ACDOC_L) and used Table /1LT/CF_E_HEADER.
B. For Replication Object:
Copied from Std. Repl Object (Project: REPL_CFIN, Subproject: REPL_CFIN_ST, Predefined Object: CFIST_ACDOC_R) and used Table /1LT/CF_E_HEADER.

4. Add Conversion Logic to Subproject.
Selected Subproject and then created a Conversion Logic by changing the Event-based Rule: SKIP_DOCUMENT. [Logic: If Logical System = SDEV then SKIP_RECORD, SDEV - Logical system for Source DEV system].

5. Generated Runtime Object by completing all the Steps for Runtime Object for Load Object.

6. Attempted to generate Runtime object for Replication Object (but FAILED with error stating that a undefined structure <IT_S> exists). On checking the Function Group generated after attempting to generate runtime objects, this structure exists and is truly undefined.

7. Advance Replication (LTRS) Settings:
Applied Filter Logic [Logical system = SDEV]

We have not setup the MTID for QAS system yet. However, when data is sent for Source DEV & Source QAS to test the FILTERS, data from both the Source system environments get transferred to CFIN DEV which shows that the FILTERS ARE NOT WORKING.

entry.title: How to open cases for end customer as an SAP Partner
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,

I hope this email finds you well.

I understand this may not be the correct channel for this inquiry; however, it appears to be the only available channel to submit my question. I kindly ask for your understanding.

Starting with the 2602 update, ByD cases must be created via SAP for Me. To submit a case, a user with the company’s S-ID needs to grant me the appropriate authorization. However, I am currently not aware of the S-ID associated with the company.

Could you please advise on how I may submit a case under this company?

Thank you very much for your assistance.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to open cases for end customer as an SAP Partner
entry.content:
Hello,

You may find the "Service Partner User" (SPU) function useful for your issue.

As a partner, you can refer to KBA 2518736 - "Service Partners: How to get authorizations for your S-user ID to create support cases on-behalf of your customers - SAP for Me".

Your customer needs to invite your S-user as written in KBA 2518600 - "How does a customer invite partner S-user IDs to create cases on their behalf using the Service Partner User functionality - SAP for Me".
(It's important to pay special attention to step 6. "Set a validity period for their engagement" or otherwise the functionality will not work.)

If you need any further assistance, you may contact SAP Partner Support as per KBA 1848459 - How do SAP Partners get help with all partner topics and applications (including Partner Dashboards in SAP for Me)

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Compatibity of S/4HANA 2025 SLT with Sidecar HANA
entry.content:

Thanks for contacting SLT support team. 

SLT compatibility with DB/OS depends on Not SLT but NetWeaver. You can check that in PAM, as it is mentioned in KBA 3458950 - DMIS compatibility with DB/OS - SLT
